mnemonics: Russell's Barber: A barber shaves all those who do not shave themselves. Who shaves the barber? This paradox shows a set cannot be a member of itself.
Cantor's Infinite Hotel: Even a 'full' infinite hotel can always make room for more, illustrating that not all infinities are the same size.
Burali-Forti's Orderly Mess: The set of all ordinal numbers would itself be an ordinal number, but a larger one must always exist, creating a paradox.
keyPoints: Russell's Paradox demonstrates that the 'set of all sets that are not members of themselves' leads to a logical contradiction, proving not all collections are sets.
Cantor's Paradox shows the 'set of all sets' has a cardinality greater than itself, as its power set must be larger, which is impossible.
The Burali-Forti Paradox proves the 'set of all ordinal numbers' would have to be an ordinal number greater than itself, which is a contradiction.
commonMistakes: Confusing Russell's Paradox (self-reference) with Cantor's Paradox (cardinality of the power set).
Assuming these paradoxes mean set theory is 'wrong' rather than that it needs careful axiomatic foundations (like ZFC).
Thinking these are just abstract puzzles with no consequence; they fundamentally shaped the rigorous foundations of modern mathematics.
